topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The pristine white of the printed page seemed to mock me. I stared at the screen, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. It was *my* essay. Every carefully chosen anecdote, every nuanced reflection on my passion for astrophysics, laid bare, perfectly formatted, sitting under the banner: "Sample Application Essay: Excellence in STEM." My hands trembled as I reread the opening paragraph, the words feeling like a physical assault. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, turning my skin prickling. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet room. I stood up, pacing the small space like a caged animal, my breathing shallow and quick. I needed to find the source, needed to know who had stolen this private piece of my intellectual self and turned it into some generic template for other hopefuls.

[story 2]

"This is a joke," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I picked up the PDF, the paper cool against my fingertips, and immediately crumpled it in my fist. The sound of the tearing paper was loud, a sharp, frustrated noise. My knuckles were white where I gripped the crumpled mess. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city below, the sprawling buildings seeming to mock my meticulous planning. A deep, simmering heat built in my chest, making my palms sweat. I wanted to scream at the injustice of it, to confront whoever had made this happen. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, strained. "How dare they treat my story like... like disposable content?"

[story 3]

I sat at my desk, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead feeling aggressively irritating. The essay was open, a damning indictment of some unseen bureaucracy. My eyes scanned the carefully constructed prose, and a violent tremor ran through my hands. I snatched a nearby pen and began furiously scribbling across the margins, the graphite scratching against the paper with unnecessary force. Each line I drew was sharp, jagged, mirroring the tightening knot in my stomach. I leaned back in my chair, letting out a long, ragged exhale that held no relief. My gaze swept the room, landing on every object—the textbooks, the framed photos—and for a moment, everything seemed tainted, overshadowed by the sheer audacity of having my vulnerability weaponized against me. I stood abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew, and walked toward the door, every step heavy with suppressed fury.
